chunk_summary: This section focuses on macronutrient targets for pediatric cholestasis, emphasizing the balance between medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) and long-chain triglycerides (LCT), alongside strategies to avoid fasting in children with chronic liver disease.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of MCT and LCT in managing pediatric cholestasis.
Learn strategies to prevent fasting-related complications in chronic liver disease.
Identify nutritional guidelines for protein-energy needs in children with cholestasis.
Explore evidence-based practices for macronutrient management in pediatric liver disease.
key_facts: Children with cholestasis benefit from a higher proportion of MCTs due to impaired fat absorption.
Avoiding prolonged fasting is critical to prevent hypoglycemia and catabolic states in pediatric liver disease.
Energy requirements in chronic liver disease may be 120-150% of the standard recommended daily allowance (RDA).
Protein intake should be optimized to prevent muscle wasting, with guidelines suggesting 1.5-2 g/kg/day in severe cases.
MCTs are absorbed directly into the portal vein, bypassing the need for bile acids, making them ideal for cholestasis management.
reference_quote: Children with chronic liver disease require tailored nutritional support, including increased energy intake and the use of MCTs to address fat malabsorption. (Source: NASPGHAN and ESPGHAN Position Paper)
document_key_topics: Avoidance of fasting in chronic liver disease
Protein-energy requirements in children with liver disease
Role of MCTs and LCTs in fat absorption
